title: 第 34 節
en: 2. Yayu, air (sabda + sparsa).
3. Te#as, fire (sabda + sparsa + rupa).
4. Ap, water (sabda + sparsa 4- rupa + rasa).
5. Pnfchivi, earth (sabda -h sparsa + rupa + rasa + gandha).
1 As yaft, the relative pronoun could hardly be used as a name, I supposed it might be meant for the indefinite pronoun ya#ka&, but this is doubtfuL THREE GTLtfAS. 2.55 Is Ptimslia an Agent?

Now follow a number of special questions, which seemed to require fuller treatment. The first is, Is the Purusha an agent, or is he not ? If Purusha were an agent, he would do good actions only, and there would not be the three different kinds of action. The three kinds of action are (i) Good conduct, called virtue (Dharma), which consists in kindness, control and restraint (of the organs), freedom from hatred, reflection, displaying of supernatural powers.
(3) But passion, anger, greed, fault-finding, violence, discontent, rudeness, shown by change of countenance, these are called indifferent conduct.
(3) Madness, intoxication, lassitude, nihilism, devotion to women; drowsiness, sloth, worthlessness, impurity, these are called bad conduct.

We see here once more that the three Gu?ias must have had originally a much wider meaning than is here described. They are here taken as purely moral qualities, whereas originally they must have had a much larger cosmic sense. They are not qualities or mere attributes at all ; they are on the contrary ingredients of Prakriti in its differentiation of good, indifferent, bad ; bright, dim and dark ; light, mobile, heavy. We see here the same narrowing of cosmical ideas which we had to point out before in the case of Buddhi and Ahamkara, and which, it seems to me, would render the original conception of the S&mkhya-philosophy quite unmeaning. We must never forget that, even when the Samkhya speaks of moral qualities, these qualities belong to nature as seen by the Purusha, never to Purusha apart from Prak?vti.
Tnree Chinas.
Whenever this triad is perceived in the world it is clear that agency belongs to the Gunas, and it follows that Purusha is not the agent.

Deceived by passion and darkness, and taking a wrong view of these Guuas which belong to Praknti, not to himself, a fool imagines that he himself is the agent, though in reality he is unable by himself to bend even a straw. Nay, he becomes an agent, as it were, foolish and intoxicated by 256 INDIAN PHILOSOPHY.

vain imagination and saying, 'All this was made by me and belongs to me/ And then it is said (in the Bhagavad-gita III, 37) : ' Acts are effected by the qualities (Gu?*as) of Prakriti in every way, but the Self (Atman), deluded by the conceit of the I (Ahamkara), imagines that the I is the agent/ Ibid. XIII, 31 :—
beginning and devoid of qualities, neither acts nor suffers, even while staying in the body/ And XIII, 39 :—
6 He sees (aright) who looks upon actions as in all respects performed by Prakriti alone, and upon the Self as never an agent/ Is Furusha one or many?

Now comes the important question, Is that Purusha one or many ? The answer to this question divides the Samkhya from the Vedanta-philosophy. The Samkhya answer is that the Purusha is clearly many, because of the variety in the acts of pleasure, pain, trouble, confusion and purifying (of race), health, Jbirth and death ; also on account of the stages in life (Asrama) and the difference of caste (Varna). If there were but one Purusha, as the Vedantins hold, then if one were happy, all would be happy ; if one were unhappy, all would be unhappy, and so on in the case of people aftected by trouble, confusion of race, purity of race, health, birth and death. Hence there is not one Purusha, but many, on account of the manifoldness indicated by form, birth, abode, fortune, society or loneliness. Thus Kapila, Asuri, Pa/?&asikha and Pata>7#ali, and all other Sa/wkhya teachers describe Purusha as many.
Ved&nta Saying's.

But teachers who follow the Vedanta, such as Harihara, Hira^yagarbha, Vyasa and others, describe Purusha as one. And why so ? Because (as the Vedanta says), i. ' Purusha is all this, what has been and what is to be,, he is lord of that immortality which springs up by (sacri- VEDANTA SAYINGS. 257 ficial) food, that is, he is beyond the immortality of the ordinary immortal gods l.
2. That is Agni, that is Vayu, that is Surya, that is JTandramas, that is pure, that is Brahman, that is water and Pra^apati 2.
3. That is true, that is immortal, it is liberation, it is the highest point, it is indestructible, it is the glory of the sun;
4.' Higher than which there is nothing else, nothing smaller, and nothing greater, the One stands like a tree planted in the sky ; by him and by the Purusha, all this is filled 3.
5. Having hands and feet everywhere, having mouth, head and eyes everywhere, hearing everywhere in this world, it stands covering everything;
6. Shining4 through the qualities (Gu?^a) of all the senses, and yet free from all the senses, the master of all, the Lord, the great refuge of all ;
7. He is all substances everywhere, the Self of all, the source of all ; that in which everything is absorbed, that the sages know as Brahman.
8. For 5 there is but one Self of beings, settled in everybody, it is seen as one and as many, like the moon in the water.
9. For he alone, the great Self, dwells in all beings, whether moving or motionless, he by whom all this was spread out.
10. This Self of the world is one — by whom was it*made manifold ? Some speak of the Self as several, because of the existence of knowledge, &c. (because knowledge is different in different people).

11. Wisee people see the same (Atman) in the Brahman, 1 These verses are meant to represent the views of the Vedanta, and they are mostly taken from the Upanishads. The first from Svet. Up. Ill, 15, occurs also Taitt. Jir. Ill, 12, i, and in the Big-veda X, 90, 2, where we should read, Ya"t annenadhir6hati, see Deussen, Geschichte, I, p. 152.
3 Mahanar^ Up. I, 7 ; cf. Va$r. Sawh. 32, i.
s Svet, Up. Ill, 9 ; Mahanar. Up. X, 20.
zh: 2. 耶誦（Yayu），空氣（聲音 + 觸覺；sabda + sparsa）。
3. 提耶阿斯（Tejas），火（聲+觸+色）。
4. Ap，水（聲音＋觸覺＋色相＋味覺）。
5. 毘紐（Prithivi），地（聲‧觸‧色‧味‧香）。
1 既然 yaft 作為關係代名詞幾乎不可能被當作名稱使用，我推測它可能是指不定代名詞 ya#ka&，但這是有疑問的。《三部吠陀》2.55 毗濕奴是施事者嗎？
現在依次提出若干特殊問題，似乎需要更充分的論述。首先是：普魯沙（Purusha）是否為行動者？如果普魯沙是行動者，他就只會進行善行，便不會存在三種不同的行動。三種行動分別為：（一）善行品德，稱為法（Dharma），包括仁慈、克制與節制（各種感官），無有怨恨、思慮深遠、展現超自然力量。
（3）然而熱情、憤怒、貪婪、好挑剔、暴力、不滿、粗魯，因面容變化而顯現者，此等謂之漠然的行為。
（3）瘋狂、酗酒、懈怠、虛無主義、溺於女色；昏睡、懶惰、無價值、不潔，這些被稱為惡劣的行為。

我們在此再次看到三種古那（Guṇa）必定原本具有遠比此處所述更廣泛的含義。它們在此處被理解為純粹的道德品質，但原本應當具有更大的宇宙意義。它們根本不是品質或單純的屬性；相反地，它們是自性（Prakṛti）在其善、無差別、惡；明亮、晦暗、黑暗；輕盈、流動、沉重的分化中的成分。我們在此看到同樣的宇宙觀念的狹隘化現象，這正是我們之前在佛地（Buddhi）與我慢（Ahaṃkāra）的情形中所必須指出的，而且在我看來，這似乎會使數論哲學（Sāṃkhya）的原初概念變得毫無意義。我們絕不能忘記，即使當數論談論道德品質時，這些品質也是屬於被普魯沙（Purusha）所見之自然的，而非屬於獨立於自性（Prakṛti）之外的普魯沙。
我收到您的指示，但英文原文僅為「Tnree Chinas.」，這似乎包含一個拼寫錯誤（「Tnree」應為「Three」）。 無論如何，這個短語的字面翻譯為： 三個中國。 但請確認這是否為穆勒《宗教學導論》（1873）中的實際段落。若您能提供完整的上下文或更正拼寫，我將能提供更準確的學術翻譯。
每當此三元性在世界中被認知時，顯然作用力（agency）屬於三德（Gunas），由此可推，原人（Purusha）並非施事者。
被激情與黑暗所欺騙，對這些屬於實性（Prakṛti）而非屬於他自身的本質（Guṇas）採取了錯誤的看法，愚人想像自己是行動者，儘管實際上他甚至無法自己折彎一根稻草。不，他變成了行動者，彷彿他是個愚昧且被激情所陶醉的人，256 印度哲學。
無謂的想象與說法，『一切這些都是由我造成的，並且屬於我』。然後在《薄伽梵歌》（Bhagavad-gita）第三章第三十七節中說道：『行為在各個方面皆由自性的質性（Guṇas）所導引，然而自我（Atman）為我執（Ahamkara）的妄想所迷惑，遂想象「我」是施行者』。同上，第十三章第三十一節：——
無始且無特質，即便居於身軀之中，亦既不作為亦不受苦。」又云《薄伽梵歌》第十三章第三十九節：——
他看得對的人，是這樣觀看的：所有的行為都完全是由實性(Prakriti)單獨執行的，而自我(Self)永遠不是行為者。神人(Furusha)是一個還是多個？

現在提出一個重要問題：那個普魯沙（Purusha）是一還是多？對這個問題的回答將數論派（Samkhya）與吠檀多哲學區分開來。數論派的答案是，普魯沙明顯是多的，因為在樂、苦、困擾、混亂與淨化（種族）、健康、出生與死亡的行為中存在著多樣性；也因為在人生階段（Asrama）與種姓（Varna）的差異中存在著多樣性。如果只有一個普魯沙，如吠檀多論者所主張的那樣，那麼若一個人幸福，所有人都會幸福；若一個人不幸，所有人都會不幸，在被困擾、種族混亂、種族淨化、健康、出生與死亡所影響的人們的情況中也是如此。因此，不是有一個普魯沙，而是有多個普魯沙，因為由形體、出生、住處、命運、社交或孤獨所顯示的繁多性。因此卡皮拉（Kapila）、阿蘇里（Asuri）、帕納西卡（Pañasikha）與帕坦伽利（Patañjali）及所有其他數論派教師都將普魯沙描述為多的。
吠檀多的言說
但是遵循吠檀多的教師，如毗濕奴·濕婆（Harihara）、金胎（Hirayagarbha）、韋亞娑（Vyasa）等人，將普魯沙（Purusha）描述為一。為什麼呢？因為（如吠檀多所言）：「普魯沙就是一切，已過去的與將來的，他是那超越凡俗不朽諸提婆的不朽性之主，那不朽性由（祭祀）食物所滋生」。
2. 那就是阿格尼（Agni），那就是瓦尤（Vayu），那就是蘇利亞（Surya），那就是月亮（Tandrama），那就是純淨者，那就是梵（Brahman），那就是水和婆羅波提（Prajāpati）²。
3. 那是真實，那是不朽，那是解脫，那是至高之點，那是不可摧毀，那是太陽的榮光；
4. 「沒有比它更高的東西，沒有比它更小的，也沒有比它更大的，這唯一者如同一棵樹植立於天空；通過它和通過原人（Purusha），一切都被充滿了」3。
5. 在這個世界中，四處有手有足，四處有口有頭有眼，四處聞聽，它屹立於此，涵蓋萬物；
6. 透過所有感官的特質（Guṇa）而光耀閃爍，卻又超越所有感官，統御萬物之主，至高之主，普遍的偉大庇護所；
他是無所不在的一切實體，是一切之自（Self），是一切之源；智者知曉一切被吸收其中之物為梵（Brahman）。
8. 對於五而言，眾生只有一個自我，安住於每一個身體之中，它被視為一與多，如水中之月。
9. 蓋唯有他，偉大的自我（ātman），住居於一切有情眾生之中，無論是運動者或不動者，正是他展布了這一切。
10. 這世界的自我是一體的——它是由誰被造成多元的？有人因為知識的存在等原因而談論自我為多個，（因為知識在不同的人身上是不同的）。
11. 智者在梵（Brahman）中看到同一者（Atman）。¹ 這些詩句旨在代表吠檀多派的觀點，多數取自奧義書。第一首出自《淨白奧義書》（Svet. Up.）III, 15，也見於《泰迪利耶梵書》（Taitt. Jir.）III, 12, i，以及《梨俱吠陀》X, 90, 2，其中應讀作「Ya"t annenadhir6hati」，參見德伊森（Deussen），《歷史》I，第152頁。
3 摩訶那若奧義書第一卷第七章；參照瓦吉桑希塔32卷第一章。
白色奧義書 III, 9；摩訶那羅延奧義書 X, 20。
